STAT508_DAA5

Ada Lazuli

February 24, 2026

Front Matter

remove(list = ls()) 
library(tidyverse)
library(gridExtra)
library(ISLR2)
library(leaps)
library(glmnet)
library(reactable) 
library(dplyr)

Problem 1 - Best Subsets Selection Using Indirect Estimate of Test Error

Part a. - Identify the best model of each size for predicting dis

data("Boston")
regfitBS <- regsubsets(dis ~ ., data = Boston, nvmax = ncol(Boston) - 1)
summary(regfitBS)
## Subset selection object
## Call: regsubsets.formula(dis ~ ., data = Boston, nvmax = ncol(Boston) - 
##     1)
## 12 Variables  (and intercept)
##         Forced in Forced out
## crim        FALSE      FALSE
## zn          FALSE      FALSE
## indus       FALSE      FALSE
## chas        FALSE      FALSE
## nox         FALSE      FALSE
## rm          FALSE      FALSE
## age         FALSE      FALSE
## rad         FALSE      FALSE
## tax         FALSE      FALSE
## ptratio     FALSE      FALSE
## lstat       FALSE      FALSE
## medv        FALSE      FALSE
## 1 subsets of each size up to 12
## Selection Algorithm: exhaustive
##           crim zn  indus chas nox rm  age rad tax ptratio lstat medv
## 1  ( 1 )  " "  " " " "   " "  "*" " " " " " " " " " "     " "   " " 
## 2  ( 1 )  " "  "*" " "   " "  "*" " " " " " " " " " "     " "   " " 
## 3  ( 1 )  " "  "*" " "   " "  "*" " " "*" " " " " " "     " "   " " 
## 4  ( 1 )  " "  "*" " "   " "  "*" " " "*" " " " " " "     " "   "*" 
## 5  ( 1 )  " "  "*" "*"   " "  "*" " " "*" " " " " " "     " "   "*" 
## 6  ( 1 )  "*"  "*" "*"   " "  "*" " " "*" " " " " " "     " "   "*" 
## 7  ( 1 )  "*"  "*" "*"   " "  "*" " " "*" " " " " " "     "*"   "*" 
## 8  ( 1 )  "*"  "*" "*"   " "  "*" " " "*" "*" " " " "     "*"   "*" 
## 9  ( 1 )  "*"  "*" "*"   "*"  "*" " " "*" "*" " " " "     "*"   "*" 
## 10  ( 1 ) "*"  "*" "*"   "*"  "*" " " "*" "*" "*" " "     "*"   "*" 
## 11  ( 1 ) "*"  "*" "*"   "*"  "*" " " "*" "*" "*" "*"     "*"   "*" 
## 12  ( 1 ) "*"  "*" "*"   "*"  "*" "*" "*" "*" "*" "*"     "*"   "*"

Part b. - Identify the best model using the BIC values

regfitBSsummary <- summary(regfitBS)

optSizeBIC <- which.min(regfitBSsummary$bic)

BICdf <- data.frame(Model = 1:nrow(regfitBSsummary$outmat), BIC = regfitBSsummary$bic)
  
ggplot(data = BICdf, mapping = aes(x = Model,y = BIC)) + geom_line() + geom_point() +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = c(1:nrow(regfitBSsummary$outmat))) + 
  geom_point(data = BICdf[optSizeBIC, ], color = "red", size = 3) +
  labs(x = "Num Features", y = "BIC")

Based on the plot, there is some ambiguity between whether 5 or 6 features would yield a better model. If 5 features are chosen, the BIC is a little worse but the model is easier to interpret. The model with 6 features has a better BIC, but the the additional feature reduces the ambiguity.

Using strictly the best BIC, the 6 features in the model would consist of:

  1. crim
  2. zn
  3. indus
  4. nox
  5. age
  6. medv

Part c. - If using adjusted r-squared, are the results different? Explain.

optSizeBIC <- which.min(regfitBSsummary$bic)
optSizeAdjR2 <- which.max(regfitBSsummary$adjr2)
AdjR2df <- data.frame(Model = 1:nrow(regfitBSsummary$outmat),
                    AdjR2 = regfitBSsummary$adjr2)
  
ggplot(data = AdjR2df, mapping = aes(x = Model, y = AdjR2)) +
  geom_line() +
  geom_point() +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = c(1:11)) + 
  labs(x = "Number of Features in the Model",
       y = expression("Adjusted " * R^2 * " Value")) +
  geom_point(data = AdjR2df[optSizeAdjR2,] , color = "red", size = 3)

If \(R^2_{adj}\) was used instead, it seems that the model with 7 features is better because it can explain more of the variability in dis.

The model with 7 features has all of the features the model chosen earlier, but also includes lstat

Problem 2 - Best Subsets Selection Using Direct Estimate of Test Error

Part a. - Perform an 80/20 training/validation split using a seed of 123.

set.seed(123)
trainInd <- sample(1:nrow(Boston), floor(0.8*nrow(Boston)), replace = FALSE)

train <- Boston[trainInd, ]
validation <- Boston[ -trainInd,]

Part b. - Use the best subsets approach to identify the best model of each size

regfitBSval <- regsubsets(dis ~ ., data = train, nvmax = ncol(Boston) - 1)
regfitBSsummary <- summary(regfitBSval)

regfitBSsummary
## Subset selection object
## Call: regsubsets.formula(dis ~ ., data = train, nvmax = ncol(Boston) - 
##     1)
## 12 Variables  (and intercept)
##         Forced in Forced out
## crim        FALSE      FALSE
## zn          FALSE      FALSE
## indus       FALSE      FALSE
## chas        FALSE      FALSE
## nox         FALSE      FALSE
## rm          FALSE      FALSE
## age         FALSE      FALSE
## rad         FALSE      FALSE
## tax         FALSE      FALSE
## ptratio     FALSE      FALSE
## lstat       FALSE      FALSE
## medv        FALSE      FALSE
## 1 subsets of each size up to 12
## Selection Algorithm: exhaustive
##           crim zn  indus chas nox rm  age rad tax ptratio lstat medv
## 1  ( 1 )  " "  " " " "   " "  "*" " " " " " " " " " "     " "   " " 
## 2  ( 1 )  " "  "*" " "   " "  "*" " " " " " " " " " "     " "   " " 
## 3  ( 1 )  " "  "*" " "   " "  "*" " " "*" " " " " " "     " "   " " 
## 4  ( 1 )  " "  "*" " "   " "  "*" " " "*" " " " " " "     " "   "*" 
## 5  ( 1 )  " "  "*" "*"   " "  "*" " " "*" " " " " " "     " "   "*" 
## 6  ( 1 )  "*"  "*" "*"   " "  "*" " " "*" " " " " " "     " "   "*" 
## 7  ( 1 )  "*"  "*" "*"   " "  "*" " " "*" " " " " " "     "*"   "*" 
## 8  ( 1 )  "*"  "*" "*"   "*"  "*" " " "*" " " " " " "     "*"   "*" 
## 9  ( 1 )  "*"  "*" "*"   "*"  "*" " " "*" "*" " " " "     "*"   "*" 
## 10  ( 1 ) "*"  "*" "*"   "*"  "*" " " "*" "*" " " "*"     "*"   "*" 
## 11  ( 1 ) "*"  "*" "*"   "*"  "*" "*" "*" "*" " " "*"     "*"   "*" 
## 12  ( 1 ) "*"  "*" "*"   "*"  "*" "*" "*" "*" "*" "*"     "*"   "*"
optSizeBIC <- which.min(regfitBSsummary$bic)

BICdf <- data.frame(Model = 1:nrow(regfitBSsummary$outmat), BIC = regfitBSsummary$bic)
  
ggplot(data = BICdf, mapping = aes(x = Model,y = BIC)) + geom_line() + geom_point() +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = c(1:nrow(regfitBSsummary$outmat))) + 
  geom_point(data = BICdf[optSizeBIC, ], color = "red", size = 3) +
  labs(x = "Num Features", y = "BIC")

After training on the training set, there is ambiguity between whether 6 or 7 features should be used. Performance on the validation will be used to make the final decision.

Part c. - What is the optimal number of variables that should be included based on the validation set MSE?

validationMat <- model.matrix(dis ~ ., data = validation)

numModels <- ncol(validationMat) - 1 
valMSE <- rep(NA, numModels)

for (i in 1:numModels){
  coefi <- coef(regfitBSval, id = i)
  yhat <- validationMat[, names(coefi)] %*% coefi
  valMSE[i] <- mean((validation$dis - yhat)^2) 
}

optSizeVal <- which.min(valMSE)

valMSEdf <- data.frame(Model = 1:numModels,
                       MSE = valMSE)

ggplot(data = valMSEdf, mapping = aes(x = Model, y = MSE)) +
  geom_line() +
  geom_point() +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = c(1:11)) + 
  labs(x = "Number of Features in the Model",
       y = "Validation Set MSE") +
  geom_point(data = valMSEdf[optSizeVal,] , color = "red", size = 3)

Based on the results of Validation set MSE, the optimal number of features is 6. The 6 features would still be

  1. crim
  2. zn
  3. indus
  4. nox
  5. age
  6. medv

Problem 3 - Shrinkage/Regularization Methods

Part a - Prepare the data. Include an 80/20 training/validation split using a seed of 123

Xmat <- model.matrix(dis ~ ., data = Boston)[, -1]
yvec <- Boston$dis
set.seed(123)
trainInd <- sample(1:nrow(Boston), floor(0.8*nrow(Boston)), replace = FALSE)
set.seed(NULL)
XmatTrain <- Xmat[trainInd,]
XmatVal <- Xmat[-trainInd,]
yvecTrain <- yvec[trainInd]
yvecVal <- yvec[-trainInd]

Part b. - Create plots of the coefficient paths for LASSO and Ridge

lassoModel <- glmnet(x = XmatTrain, y = yvecTrain,
                    family = "gaussian",
                    alpha = 1,
                    lambda = NULL,
                    standardize = TRUE)

#Create a plot of the coefficient paths
plot(lassoModel, xvar = "lambda", label = TRUE, sub = "LASSO Coefficient Paths")

ridgeModel <- glmnet(x = XmatTrain, y = yvecTrain,
                     family = "gaussian",
                     alpha = 0, 
                     lambda = NULL,
                     standardize = TRUE)

#Create a plot of the coefficient paths
plot(ridgeModel, xvar = "lambda", label = TRUE, sub = "Ridge Coefficient Paths")

Part c. - For LASSO, choose the optimal value of the tuning parameter, lambda.

set.seed(123)
lassoCV <- cv.glmnet(x = XmatTrain, y = yvecTrain,
                    family = "gaussian",
                    alpha = 1, 
                    lambda = NULL,
                    standardize = TRUE,
                    nfolds = 10)
set.seed(NULL)

#Show the LASSO CV results
plot(lassoCV)

There are a couple of methods for choosing lambda. Based on the 1 standard error rule, the optimal value is 0.1000188. The optimal value of lambda based on the min rule is 0.0187417

Part d. - Using the value of lambda associated with the 1se rule, what variables remain in the LASSO model

coefLam1se <- predict(lassoCV, s = lassoCV$lambda.1se, type = "coefficients")
tempdf <- data.frame(Variable = row.names(coefLam1se), lam1se = as.numeric(coefLam1se))
reactable(tempdf %>% filter(abs(lam1se) > 0), searchable = FALSE, compact = TRUE, striped = TRUE, defaultPageSize = 13, showPageSizeOptions = FALSE)

The variables in the lasso model are:

  1. crim
  2. zn
  3. indus
  4. nox
  5. age
  6. medv

Part e. - Using the LASSO model with optimal lambda based on the 1se rule, what is the validation set RMSE?

lassoYhat <- predict(lassoCV, s = lassoCV$lambda.min, newx = XmatVal) 
lassoMSE <-  mean((yvecVal - lassoYhat)^2)
lassoRMSE <- sqrt(lassoMSE)
lassoRMSE
## [1] 1.021331

The lasso RMSE based on the validation set is: 1.0213312

Part f. - Compare validation set RMSE values for various methods

set.seed(123) # not asked for, but for repeatability
ridgeCV <- cv.glmnet(x = XmatTrain, y = yvecTrain,
                    family = "gaussian",
                    alpha = 0, 
                    lambda = NULL,
                    standardize = TRUE,
                    nfolds = 10)
ridgeYhat <- predict(ridgeCV, s = ridgeCV$lambda.min, newx = XmatVal) 
ridgeMSE <-  mean((yvecVal - ridgeYhat)^2)
ridgeRMSE <- sqrt(ridgeMSE)

train <- Boston[trainInd, ]
validation <- Boston[-trainInd,]
lsModel <- lm(dis ~ crim + zn + indus + nox + age + medv, data = train)
lsYhat <- predict(lsModel, newdata = validation)

lsMSE <- mean((validation$dis - lsYhat)^2)
lsRMSE <- sqrt(lsMSE)
data.frame(metric = "Validation RMSE", "Lasso" = lassoRMSE, "Ridge"= ridgeRMSE, "Least Squares" = lsRMSE)
##            metric    Lasso    Ridge Least.Squares
## 1 Validation RMSE 1.021331 1.028428      1.011486

LASSO and Ridge produced very similar RMSE scores on the validation set, with LASSO performing marginally better. The Lease Squares method produced the lowest RMSE score on the validation set.
